title: Skeletal System
subtitle: 206 bones that form the structural framework of the human body

bones.name: Cranium
bones.description: The skull cap that protects the brain. Composed of several fused bones forming a rigid shell.
bones.funFact: The cranium is made of 8 fused bones, and at birth the gaps (fontanelles) haven't closed yet — that's the 'soft spot' on a baby's head.

bones.name: Mandible
bones.description: The lower jaw bone. The largest and strongest bone of the face, it holds the lower teeth and enables chewing.
bones.funFact: The mandible is the only skull bone that can move — everything else is fused together.

bones.name: Clavicle
bones.description: The collarbone. A long, thin bone connecting the arm to the torso.
bones.funFact: The clavicle is the most commonly broken bone in the human body.

bones.name: Scapula
bones.description: The shoulder blade. A large, triangular bone on the upper back that anchors arm muscles.
bones.funFact: Your scapula isn't directly attached to your ribcage — it floats in a sea of muscles!

bones.name: Sternum
bones.description: The breastbone. A flat, dagger-shaped bone in the center of the chest that connects the ribs.
bones.funFact: The sternum is where CPR compressions are applied — it can withstand up to 100 kg of force.

bones.name: Rib Cage
bones.description: 12 pairs of curved bones forming the thoracic cage, protecting the heart and lungs.
bones.funFact: You have 12 pairs of ribs — the bottom 2 are 'floating ribs' that don't attach to the sternum.

bones.name: Humerus
bones.description: The upper arm bone extending from shoulder to elbow. The longest bone of the arm.
bones.funFact: "Humerus" literally means "upper arm" in Latin. Yes, it's related to the word 'humorous' because it's funny… bone.

bones.name: Radius
bones.description: The forearm bone on the thumb side. Wider at the wrist than the ulna.
bones.funFact: The radius rotates around the ulna when you twist your wrist — that's what allows palm-up and palm-down motion.

bones.name: Ulna
bones.description: The forearm bone on the pinky side. Forms the elbow joint with the humerus.
bones.funFact: The bump you feel at your elbow (the 'funny bone') is actually the ulna's olecranon process, not a bone you can break easily.

bones.name: Pelvis
bones.description: The hip bone. A basin-shaped ring of bones supporting the spine and protecting abdominal organs.
bones.funFact: The female pelvis is wider and more oval than the male pelvis — evolved for childbirth.

bones.name: Femur
bones.description: The thigh bone. The longest, strongest, and heaviest bone in the human body.
bones.funFact: The femur is so strong it can support up to 30x your body weight. It takes a car collision to break one.

bones.name: Patella
bones.description: The kneecap. A small, triangular bone embedded in the quadriceps tendon protecting the knee joint.
bones.funFact: The patella is a sesamoid bone — it grows inside a tendon rather than connecting to other bones via joints.

bones.name: Tibia
bones.description: The shin bone. The larger, weight-bearing bone of the lower leg, second only to the femur in size.
bones.funFact: The tibia is the most common site for stress fractures in runners. Also — 'tibia' means 'flute' in Latin.

bones.name: Fibula
bones.description: The thin bone of the lower leg, running alongside the tibia. Does not bear weight.
bones.funFact: The fibula doesn't bear weight — its main job is anchoring muscles. You can donate one and still walk normally.

bones.name: Carpals
bones.description: The eight small bones forming the wrist, arranged in two rows of four.
bones.funFact: The word 'carpal' comes from Greek 'karpos' meaning wrist. Carpal tunnel syndrome happens right here.

bones.name: Metacarpals
bones.description: The five long bones in the palm of the hand, connecting the wrist to the fingers.
bones.funFact: Your knuckles are the ends of metacarpals — the second and third metacarpals are the ones that 'crack' the loudest.

bones.name: Phalanges (Fingers)
bones.description: The finger bones. 14 bones per hand — each finger has 3 (proximal, middle, distal), the thumb has 2.
bones.funFact: Despite being tiny, phalanges are some of the most densely innervated bones — that's why fingers are so sensitive.

bones.name: Tarsals
bones.description: The seven bones of the ankle and hindfoot, including the heel bone (calcaneus) and talus.
bones.funFact: The calcaneus (heel bone) is the largest tarsal and absorbs the impact of every step you take — about 1.5x your body weight per step.

bones.name: Metatarsals
bones.description: The five long bones in the midfoot, connecting the ankle to the toes.
bones.funFact: The second metatarsal is the longest and most commonly fractured in dancers — known as 'dancer's fracture'.
